element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Provides 10x10 room matrix; highlights princess location for navigation guidance
Required for door progression; alignment puzzle mechanic core to advancement
Player must carry matching colored key; adds inventory/progression constraint
Specialized puzzle object for vertical traversal and spatial manipulation
Avoidable; can be killed with sword/dagger in NES version if timing is precise
Increased danger; requires evasion or sword combat with tight timing windows
Most dangerous enemies; require advanced combat or evasion tactics
Close-range combat mechanic; enemy must be directly adjacent; mistiming causes player damage

typical_relief_triggers: Solving puzzle block alignment and proceeding to next room
Obtaining color-matched key and unlocking door
Finding safe corridor between enemy zones
typical_tension_triggers: Puzzle rooms requiring precise multi-object alignment (time-consuming)
Having to stand still by door to open it (vulnerability window)
Close-range sword combat with enemies (high timing risk)
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Puzzle-paced with deliberate timing. Logic challenges dominate over action reflexes. Tension derives from spatial constraint puzzles and enemy avoidance rather than reflex speed. High intellectual friction in individual rooms; relief through progression to new area.

difficulty_curve.early_game: Tutorial-paced: introduction of map, basic pushable objects, simple color-key progression. Safe exploration encouraged.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Complexity escalation: larger puzzle rooms with multiple objects requiring precise alignment. Introduction of multiple enemy types. Increased spatial awareness demand.
difficulty_curve.late_game: High-difficulty enemy concentration (Fire Spirits, Vikings, Phantom Flowers). Complex multi-step puzzles. Sword combat becomes necessary risk. No save feature in NES version creates endurance demand.
notable_spikes: First encounter with time-consuming multi-object alignment puzzle (room reset can be frustrating)
Introduction of close-range sword combat mechanic (high skill floor due to timing precision)
Overall NES difficulty spike vs. Famicom (50 lives in NES but no save/continue; Famicom had save support)

notable_level_patterns.description: Player must push brick blocks, barrels, candles, and elevator blocks into precise positions to unlock doors. Can only open doors while standing adjacent to them (not mid-jump). Often time-consuming and requires spatial planning.
Colored doors require matching colored keys carried by player. Forces inventory management and backtracking to retrieve specific keys before proceeding.

Horizontal scrolling rooms populated with enemy types. Player must navigate through or around enemies using sword combat (NES version) or object throwing (MSX version). Close-range melee requires precise timing.
Elevator Controlling Blocks and other specialized objects enable vertical and spatial transitions. Require puzzle solving before activation.
